title: Tomato Sauce on Cotton
stainChemistry: Tomato sauce is a three-component stain: lycopene (the red carotenoid pigment, fat-soluble), plant tannins (polyphenols), and oil (olive oil + pan oils used in cooking). Lycopene is what makes tomato stains stubborn — it's a fat-soluble dye that gets locked onto the fiber by the oil carrier, just like mustard's curcumin but darker and less UV-responsive. Cotton is pure cellulose, tolerant of IPA, NSD, 3% H₂O₂, and hot post-chemistry wash. The ordering rule is oil-first (strip the carrier) then tannin (disrupt hydrogen bonds) then oxidizer (break the lycopene chromophore) — skip the oil strip and the rest of the sequence plateaus.
whyThisWorks: Oil carrier must come off first or the rest of the chemistry can't reach the lycopene. NSD (pro) or Dawn (home) pulls the oil. Acid-side tannin spotter disrupts the plant-tannin hydrogen bonds. 3% H₂O₂ oxidizes the residual lycopene chromophore; sunlight accelerates it because lycopene is mildly photosensitive. Hot wash finishes. This is the same oil-then-tannin-then-oxidizer pattern as red wine's sibling stains.
defaultAssumption: Fresh tomato sauce on cotton is a high-confidence DIY; aged tomato sauce (overnight+) may need a peroxide + sunlight cycle in addition to the oil + tannin pass. Heat-set (ironed or hot-dried) climbs into pro territory.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Dull blade, cold water, white cotton terry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Scrape solid sauce off with a dull blade — don't rub. Cold flush from the BACK of the fabric. Cold only — heat combined with the oil carrier drives lycopene deeper into cotton and makes subsequent chemistry less effective.

Apply NSD, work with a soft bristle brush. The oil carrier (olive oil, pan oils) must come off before peroxide can reach the pigment. Tamp, don't scrub hard. Rinse cool. Repeat if oil residue is still visible.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Professional tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter or PRENETT-A) — acid-side tannin treatment
spottingProtocol.instruction: Acid-side tannin spotter by dropper on the residual color. Disrupts the tannin-cellulose hydrogen bond. Dwell 3–5 min, blot. Repeat once if shadow persists.
Apply 3% H₂O₂ to residual lycopene shadow. For aged stains, hang the damp garment in sunlight — UV accelerates lycopene oxidation. Fresh stains usually clear without the sun step.
Rinse until water runs clear. Launder hot per care label.

steps: 1. Scrape bulk. Cold flush from the back.
2. NSD — strip the oil carrier first. Tamp, not scrub.
3. Acid-side tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter / PRENETT-A). Dropper, 3–5 min.
4. 3% H₂O₂ on residual lycopene. Sunlight assist on aged stains.
5. NSD flush, hot wash per care label.
products: Professional tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter or PRENETT-A)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold through spotting; hot wash post-chemistry; sun during dry for aged
warnings: Oil carrier off FIRST — peroxide can't reach lycopene otherwise
Never use hot water before the oil is stripped — drives pigment deeper
One oxidizer at a time, NSD-flushed between

homeSolutions.instruction: Lift solid sauce off the fabric with a dull butter knife or credit card edge. Don't rub — rubbing drives the sauce into the weave. Scrape what will come off easily.
Hold the BACK of the stained area under cold running water. The sauce pushes out the way it came in. Cold only — hot water drives the oily red pigment deeper into cotton.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn First — Before Any Color Work
homeSolutions.instruction: Tomato sauce has an oil carrier (olive oil, the pan oil it was cooked in). That oil protects the red pigment from everything else until the oil is removed. Work a drop of Dawn dish soap into the stain with a fingertip or soft toothbrush, let sit 10 minutes, rinse cool. You should see the stain lighten noticeably once the oil is gone.

homeSolutions.agent: White Vinegar Dab for Stubborn Red
homeSolutions.instruction: Mix 1 teaspoon white vinegar with 4 tablespoons cool water. Dab onto residual red with a white cloth, let sit 3 minutes, blot. The vinegar's mild acidity breaks the tannin hydrogen bond. Repeat once if needed.

For stubborn residual color: dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide on the spot, let it bubble 3–5 min. For aged stains, hang the damp shirt in direct sunlight for 30–60 min — UV helps fade the lycopene pigment. Skip on dark or dyed cotton.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia — toxic fume risk.

homeSolutions.agent: Hot Wash, Inspect Before the Dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. Inspect BEFORE the dryer — dryer heat sets any residual lycopene permanently. If you still see orange, repeat the Dawn + peroxide round.

If the sauce has been hot-dried or two home rounds haven't cleared it, take it to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Tomato sauce on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with Dawn + vinegar + 3% hydrogen peroxide + sun."

steps: 1. Scrape solid sauce off with a dull blade — don't rub.
2. Cold water flush from the BACK of the fabric.
3. Dawn dish soap FIRST — strips the oil carrier. Dwell 10 min, rinse cool.
4. Diluted white vinegar dab for residual red. 3 min, blot.
5. 3% hydrogen peroxide + 30–60 min sunlight on stubborn stains. Skip on dark cotton.
6. Hot wash per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer.
7. Escalate if heat-set or two rounds failed.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if tomato sauce has been through a hot dryer or iron, if two home rounds don't clear it, or if the cotton is dark dyed and peroxide isn't safe.
warnings: Oil first, then color — peroxide can't work through the oil carrier
Cold water during spotting — heat drives the oily pigment deeper
Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia
Inspect before the dryer — heat sets lycopene permanently

neverDo: Never hot-water-flush tomato sauce before the oil is stripped — drives lycopene deeper
Never skip the Dawn/NSD step — the oil protects the dye from everything else
Never layer oxidizers — one at a time
Never dry with residual chemistry in the fiber
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates NSD, acid-side spotters, 3% H₂O₂, sunlight, hot wash
Dyed cotton: skip or inside-seam-test peroxide
Heat-safe fiber once chemistry is cleared

materialWarnings: Oil carrier off first, then color work
Cold during spotting, hot at wash
Peroxide on whites; test inside seam on dark cotton

escalation.when: Tomato sauce heat-set by iron or hot dryer, two home rounds failed, or dark dyed cotton where peroxide isn't safe.
escalation.whatToTell: "Tomato sauce on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with Dawn + vinegar + 3% H₂O₂ + sunlight. [Hot-washed / not yet laundered.]"

contextQuestions: Has it been through a hot wash or dryer?
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
Was the sauce oily (pan-cooked, restaurant-style) or lean (jarred marinara straight)? Oilier = harder.
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Lycopene + oil carrier is the engine. Remove the oil and the rest of the chemistry can reach the pigment; skip it and everything stalls at faint orange. Sunlight + 3% peroxide is a patient finisher for aged residual.
